speaker: interviewer
text: It's the 17th of September, 1995. And we're in Sydney, Australia. Camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So basically, the start of the war in 1939 and the Hungarian entry into the war in 1941 didn't have any major impact on your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. We were-- I-- I can quite clearly recall, We were coming home from [? Buda, ?] one of my-- of our going outs with mom-- and we seen a big placard with a big, [? mark ?] watch. And then, they told about us that there could be aerial raids on Budapest, et cetera. And couple of days later, the first planes came in. And they thrown down just lighting, you know, to light up, to take pictures. And mom was absolutely upset, because it turned out that grandma and grandpa was standing out in the middle of the street to watch it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's how the war started. And then there was putting up, fixing up under the house to be able to go there if bombing raid starts, and et cetera. But life didn't change much.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have very light recollection that, going into the army. But only for a-- for some reason, it was only for a short time. I know it was somewhere out in the country. And we went once to visit him, but it didn't-- it didn't left any deep-- deep memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was able to go into the Army, even though he was Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would the fact-- would him leaving have had anything to do with the fact that he was Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't-- I can't recall. I don't-- I don't remember, clearly. I only know that he went away for a-- a short while [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- what year would that have been? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wouldn't know. It could have been '41, '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when-- when you got the announcement that the war had started, was that a scary time for you at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't think I-- I knew-- I realized the-- what it is. And Dad tried to keep it on a very low key and explaining it that, Luke, it's very chance that the bombing would do any harm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there ever any bombing in your area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. There was a couple of houses was bombed on the other side of the academy. And also, the anti-air guns, they're working at the academy. So we heard them shooting at the planes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was that like, to have bombs coming and-- and shooting around you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very exciting. It wasn't-- it wasn't-- for some reason, it wasn't scary.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was more of an adventure?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at this time, what were you hearing of the situation regarding the war end, regarding the Jewish people in other countries in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look. Look. The-- the only thing what stays in memory, that one morning, a gentlemen turned up who turned out to be a cousin coming from Yugoslavia. And he was telling stories what happening. Naturally, not for the children. And then, the heads of the families, my father, his brother, the brother-in-law [? Berti ?] [? Bocce ?] and Arpad, and those, got together to listen to him, what he is saying, what happening with the Jews in Yugoslavia. He brought with himself one yellow star, what he used to wear. And the family listened to it and agreed that, oh, yes, these sort of things can happen in Yugoslavia, but not in-- in-- in-- in Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, Dad and [? Berti Bocce ?] went up to one of the old Army friends who was very close to the Hungarian governor, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], and told him that these are sort of stories, what we heard. What is his opinion? And he assured my father that nothing like this can happen in Hungary. We are not that way, like the stupid Yugoslavs. And my father accepted that and decided that there is no reason why to leave Hungary. That is where we're born, and that is where we should die. He didn't make it. [EMOTIONAL] [SIGHS] Was that the first time that you had seen a yellow star?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yellow star came only much later in Hungary. That was my 12th birthday present. Yellow star started in 1944, second of April, in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.,

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans came in Marchy.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll talk about that a bit later. But so, at that time, were there many refugees coming to Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look. Maybe, yes. I only know of this man. He came. And some way, my father fixed up that he got some-- supplied with some papers. And he was working in our factory till, through his papers, he got called up to a work battalion.

speaker: interviewer
text: A labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Labor. Labor battalions. Because by that time, every able-bodied man went into the Army. The Jews didn't went into normal fighting units, but went to labor battalions.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know what his name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember his name. And we haven't heard of him, after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you at all aware at that time, when you must have been around 11 or 12-- was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep.

speaker: interviewer
text: --of any of the specifics of what he was telling the older people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Not really. It wasn't something what concerned the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about through the media? Were there stories there of anything that was happening to Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I don't think so. It wasn't something what they publicized. We didn't know about, even later on, what's happening in-- in-- that there is such thing as concentration camp. And we knew that there are people who were taken out of Hungary because they were not Hungarian origin, but they were Polish, and Slovakians, and I don't know what. And they were taken to-- to places where they work. But that was something very slightly known, only in-- particularly, not for-- not for-- not for the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So other than that, basically, it was just a normal life at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were no actions, or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So have you got any particularly significant memories that stand out from these years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not till-- what-- what years are we talking about? I have very--

speaker: interviewer
text: From '41 to '44, say.

speaker: interviewee
text: '41 to '44, it was very normal, a quiet sort of life. It's not-- nothing what-- what stays in the memory of it for an 11-year-old kid. I knew that, instead of they're going for to be soldiers, they're going into work battalions. It didn't mean too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not-- not visibly. So don't forget, we were very sheltered. We were taken to school. In the school, we went to Jewish high school. All right. By-- by '44, the-- our very nice, big school in-- in [NON-ENGLISH] was taken over by the Army, but it happened with other schools also. And we had to share a school at [NON-ENGLISH] with the Jewish other high school. And one week, we went in the morning. One week, we went in the afternoon to school. And there were restrictions that there wasn't enough heating. But that went in all schools. Hungary was at war, and they needed extra hospitals, and barracks, and et cetera. For that, they used the schools. They picked on the Jewish school, that's quite normal. They picked on the other ones too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about food and that sort of thing? Was there any rationing at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think, by that time, there was some rationing. But because my father was in the-- connected with the milk industry, we always had exchange. You want milk cans? OK. You get the milk cans. That's how much it costs, plus five-- five kilo butter. And that became the visa, what you used. For the butter, we could get this and that. There was no-- there was no food problems for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did the situation actually begin to change then?

speaker: interviewee
text: The situation began to change in-- in-- in April, when the yellow star came on, when restrictions started on what hours you can be out. And then, things started to move in a very fast space. Then the--

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was after the German occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The Germans came in-- in the 19th of March, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I seen them coming through on the [NON-ENGLISH]. I was going to school. And I see--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] is-- is one was a main roads of Budapest. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the sights and sounds that you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: An army marching. It wasn't any different than when we see the Hungarian Army marching here and there. I-- for me, it didn't have any special relevance or-- or-- or meaning.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it wasn't scary?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No, you-- it's-- it's-- it's our allies, the Germans, who-- who came in to-- to-- to help. And the war was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how soon after they came in did the changes come about? That was really in April, was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The changes come about quite fast. Different Jewish orders came out. I couldn't recall them. The only thing what I remember, that the yellow star came in the second of April. And that was something what really sticks with you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about that? How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Frightening, and to being stamped, and then, not allowed to go out when you want to, but only in-- in certain hours. And restrictions started. They took the radios away. Then they switched off the telephones. Communications became very difficult. Something what you are quite normal to lift up the phone and ring up my momma, grandmother, or auntie, and suddenly, it started to became very hard. And after that, straightaway came that they wanted to put the Jews as much together as possible. And they started to name and organize, so-called, Jewish houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we talk about that, I'm just interested to know, when-- when these new laws came into being, how were you told about them? For instance, with the yellow star, what was said to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they explained it that, from now on, we have to wear the yellow star, something like that. Us-- us easy, to-- to-- to try to make it natural, not to frighten the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they specifically made that effort?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to frighten the children. Naturally, that was always was underlined. Whatever happens, happens. The children shouldn't be frightened. And it's-- it's-- it's not the children business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the order-- so who was trying not to frighten the children? The Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: The parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: The parents? Right. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The parents. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So basically, you just found out from your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look. We-- we didn't-- we didn't have anything to do with the Germans. They have enough, like you see in Hungary, who were doing it in Hungarian. So the Germans-- we-- we didn't know that there is a-- a German SS Command [NON-ENGLISH] sitting somewhere at head office. It wasn't a known fact. Horthy wasn't-- Horthy was the governor of Hungary-- wasn't a very big philo-Semite, but he did only what he had to, politically. That I learned later on. He tried to-- to keep Hungary Hungarian, and not to give totally in to the Germans, but he had to.

speaker: interviewer
text: So philo-Semite is-- what-- what is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's not-- he was not a philo-Semite.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So now, the-- basically, the parents were trying not to scare you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. I think so. We didn't see things as darkly as it-- as it looked. I haven't got any bad vibrations from that time, no-- nothing-- nothing specially bad things to-- to-- to to remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still able to go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: For a very short time, and then they closed down the school. We were not allowed to go. Look. In our street, to play with the kids, and this and that, everybody knew we are Jews before we wore the star too. My-- my parents lived there. My grandparents lived there. And with a name like Deutsche, everybody knew that you are Jewish. And they knew that we are Jewish. The mezuzah was on the door and everything. And they know very well what does it mean.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is a mezuzah?

speaker: interviewee
text: A mezuzah? A mezuzah is that thing what is on the Jewish doors. A mezuzah is a mezuzah. How can-- how can I explain it? A mezuzah is a mezuzah, what is attached to every door in a Jewish house.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. So things were not too difficult for you at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like not to have to go to school anymore? Was that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was-- it was near the end of the school year anyway. The school year finishes in June. And we finished a week earlier. And by that time, it was very hectic to go to school anyway, because there were air raids. And the air raids, they had to clean out the schools. And we have to go to this people we know, that people we know. That if you have to go to the bunkers, then there is space in your bunker, because the schools didn't had that. So it was staying at home, reading books, playing around, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the anti-Semitism become any more evident at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that very lot. Not that very lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents ever talk about it at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in front of the children. And if it was in front of the children, then they talked German, that we don't understand it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you were saying before about the houses. Can you tell me again about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They were putting together certain houses marked with a-- a yellow star. And they were putting the people into it. And that was the job of the Jewish council to organize it. They decreed which houses will be Jewish houses, and they had to fit the people in there. And then, I volunteered and worked for them, because there was no telephones so much, as a boy on a bicycle who rushes with the messages from here to there, and take notes, and helping organizing that big movement. In our house, our house became a Jewish house. And grandma, grandpa moved in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother's parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's parents. My mother, younger sister, who was still single, and my mother, other young sister with the son-- that's [? Berge ?] and Peter-- and my mother's brother's wife, Roszi. Arpad was already in work battalion. [? Berge's ?] husband, [? Pali, ?] was in work battalion. And so, in the house where we used to live the four of us, now it was grandma, grandpa, Magda, [? Berge, ?] Peter, Roszi. Became 10 of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it very cramped?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look. Nice people fit into small places, naturally, it wasn't very convenient. We had-- basically, it was a normal size room, and a very big room, and a kitchen and a bathroom. It was reasonably crowded. And also, they wanted to empty their homes, so all the furnishing came down. Luckily, we had big storage area. Because by that time, the factory wasn't working. So there was all the furnishing. Grandparents had a very nice, big house. [? Berge ?] had nice single-bedroom home. All this furnishing came down to our place. And plus our furnishing stayed there. And that's how we started to-- to live and organize the life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did everybody deal with having to do that? Did you try and make light of it and joke about it with everybody cramped in there together? Or was it kind of depressing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look. We kids were very happy, because we had the whole yard. And-- and everybody was around us. And it didn't bother us that we can't go out to the park to play. We had plenty of playing space at home. I don't think it-- it bothered us too much. It was-- it was more worry for our parents. But our kids, it wasn't so much so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what month was that again?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- that was, I think, May, end of May, that was. And then, the next thing what-- what I would remember is losing my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we talk about that, could we just go back and talk about the Jewish council and-- and what you knew about that as a young child, and then also your job as the messenger boy? So first of all, the Jewish council, what involvement did you have with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish council was doing the things what the government pushed them-- pushed them to do. And I think the idea was that, if you don't do it, we will. And they did it. They-- they organized the Jewish houses and organized the-- the people who did not live in-- in Jewish houses to move in. And those who lived in Jewish houses to, instead of using two rooms, go into one, so that they can give the free houses to those who are bombed out, was the story. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you view the people in the council? And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Look. I don't-- I didn't know the people in the council. There was one elderly gentlemen who told me that, now, you go to this street and tell these people to do this or that, or take this letter from here to there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 12 years old, you know? So it-- it was a very scary thing to go in into people's homes and have a look how big it is, their-- their flat, you know? That they have two rooms, or three rooms, and how big is the room. Because that's how they picked up the data and organized it that, yes, it's a small room. We can only put two people there. Or is it a big room? And we can put a bigger family in it, what is two adults and two children. So that's what you were doing? You were giving them the information about the different houses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I was the-- the eyes of the-- of the council, because I had a bicycle. And I was able to run around easy. And then, by that time, youngster-- young-- the young people were in-- in work battalions. And so there were only a few elderly people who were running the whole thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did people ever ask you to say that maybe they had a different amount of rooms than they actually had? Or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. [LAUGHS] Most-- most probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think you would have done it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. The rules are rules. And my father always told me that the straight and narrow, that's the way to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was--

speaker: interviewee
text: And also, I-- I find myself very important, and I wouldn't-- I wouldn't let people tell me what to do. I was-- you know, at 12 years old, it's quite a heavy, heavy thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it scary?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: But at this time, with the whole situation sort of so shaken up, to be racing between all the different houses, that wasn't something which phased you at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. It wasn't any particular problems. I knew that, if the siren goes, then you go to the nearest bunker. And that's it. But it was summertime. It was sunshine. And so? Grandma moves in. It wasn't-- it wasn't a catastrophic for us kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you doing the messenger boy job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six, eight weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Now, you were saying that the next significant memory for you was about your father. Can you tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's sister and husband, [? Berti, ?] they lived next door to us. One of their son was in labor camp. The other one went underground and was a member of the resistance. And they were trying to catch him, and they couldn't. So at the end, one night, they came with two guys and took my auntie and uncle away. And then after weeks and weeks, we find out that they are in an inter-- internation--

speaker: interviewer
text: Interrogation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Inter-- internation-- in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Intern camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: Or interrogation?

speaker: interviewee
text: After interrogation, in a-- in a special camp near Budapest. And food was very scare there. And we were allowed to send in to each of them daily a little quarter kilogram pack. Naturally, my father sent it very religiously, every day. And because we didn't want it to be on the street too much, et cetera-- so there was a boy next door, not a Jewish boy, who everyday took it for-- I don't know-- five [NON-ENGLISH]-- took it every day and posted it. So it made sure that they, at least, have some food. And on that day, he didn't felt like it, or something, so my father took the parcel to the post. And in a half an hour later, people started to tell us that the military police grabbed my father. Now, not much later, we got a message from one of the uncles what-- that, don't go out on the streets, because they're picking up people from the street. It was too late. That was the time that they were deporting the outer parts of Budapest, what's called Kispest, and Ujpest. And somehow, the numbers didn't fit, or something. And they started to pick up people on the street in Budapest, what never happened before. As I mentioned, we were living on the other side of the [NON-ENGLISH]. And the military police was stationed there. They were brought up from the country, and they were picking up people. And they picked up my father in the corner, two houses away from us. At the same time, same day, they picked up the-- an auntie with her daughter, one of my sister's kindergarten friend, quite a number of people who we knew about. But all that, we only knew afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll just end it there. I might ask some questions [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's the end of tape two of the interview with Ivan Devai. It's the 17th of September, 1995. And we're in Sydney, Australia. [BACKGROUND CONVERSATION]
